name: cameramodels
documentation:
# Camera Models ![PyPI](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/cameramodels.svg) [![Build Status](https://github.com/iory/cameramodels/workflows/Run%20Tests/badge.svg?branch=main)](https://github.com/iory/cameramodels/actions)

Documentation | Python API |

Manipulate Camera Library. ## Installation ```bash pip install cameramodels ``` If you would like to use `open3d` conversion functions, ```bash pip install cameramodels[all] ```

functions.name: cameramodels.align.align_depth_to_rgb
documentation:
Align depth image to color image.

Parameters
----------
depth : numpy.ndarray
    depth image in meter order.
bgr_cameramodel : cameramodels.PinholeCameraModel
    bgr cameramodel
depth_cameramodel : cameramodels.PinholeCameraModel
    depth cameramodel
depth_to_rgb_transform : numpy.ndarray
    4x4 transformation matrix.

Returns
-------
aligned_img : numpy.ndarray
    aligned image.

functions.name: cameramodels.calib.linear.triangulate_points_from_2d_points
documentation:
Triangulates a 3D point from 2D correspondences and camera matrices.

Parameters
----------
pt2d : array-like, shape (N, 2)
    List of 2D points in homogeneous coordinates.
P : array-like, shape (N, 3, 4)
    List of camera projection matrices.

Returns
-------
point : ndarray, shape (4,)
    Triangulated 3D point in homogeneous coordinates.

Raises
------
AssertionError
    If N != len(P) or N < 2.

Notes
-----
This function implements the linear triangulation algorithm described in
"Multiple View Geometry in Computer Vision" by Hartley and Zisserman.

functions.name: cameramodels.calib.linear.triangulate
documentation:
Triangulate 3D points from corresponding 2D points in two views.

Parameters
----------
R1 : array_like, shape (3, 3)
    Rotation matrix of the first camera.
t1 : array_like, shape (3,)
    Translation vector of the first camera.
R2 : array_like, shape (3, 3)
    Rotation matrix of the second camera.
t2 : array_like, shape (3,)
    Translation vector of the second camera.
n1 : array_like, shape (N, 3)
    Corresponding 2D points in the first view.
n2 : array_like, shape (N, 3)
    Corresponding 2D points in the second view.

Returns
-------
array_like, shape (N, 3)
    Triangulated 3D points.

functions.name: cameramodels.calib.linear.z_count
documentation:
Counts the number of points in 3D space with positive z coordinate.

Counts the number of points in 3D space with positive z coordinate
after projecting them onto the camera using
the camera extrinsic parameters R and t.

Parameters
----------
R: numpy.ndarray
    3x3 rotation matrix representing the camera's orientation
    in the world frame.
t: numpy.ndarray
    3x1 translation vector representing the camera's position
    in the world frame.
Xw_Nx3: numpy.ndarray
    N x 3 matrix representing N 3D points in the world frame.

Returns
-------
int
    The number of points with a positive z coordinate after projection.

functions.name: cameramodels.calib.linear.z_test_w2c
documentation:
Perform z-test.

Perform z-test to determine whether a triangulated 3D point is in front of
the cameras or not.

Parameters
----------
R1 : array_like, shape (3, 3)
    Rotation matrix of the first camera.
t1 : array_like, shape (3,)
    Translation vector of the first camera.
R2 : array_like, shape (3, 3)
    Rotation matrix of the second camera.
t2 : array_like, shape (3,)
    Translation vector of the second camera.
n1 : array_like, shape (N, 3)
    Corresponding 2D points in the first view.
n2 : array_like, shape (N, 3)
    Corresponding 2D points in the second view.

Returns
-------
tuple
    A tuple consisting of the sign of the test result, the number
    of 3D points in front of the cameras when the points are triangulated
    with the original camera poses, and the number of 3D points in front
    of the cameras when the cameras are inverted.

functions.name: cameramodels.calib.linear.collinearity_w2c
documentation:
Construct a sparse matrix A

Construct a sparse matrix A that represents
collinearity constraint equations.

Parameters
----------
R_w2c : array_like, shape (3, 3)
    Rotation matrix from world to camera coordinate system.
n : array_like, shape (3,)
    Normal vector of the plane on which the collinearity constraints lie.
idx_v : array_like, shape (n_v,)
    Indices of visible vertices.
idx_t : array_like, shape (n_t,)
    Indices of visible triangles.
num_v : int
    Total number of vertices in the mesh.
num_t : int
    Total number of triangles in the mesh.

Returns
-------
A : sparse matrix, shape (3, (num_v + num_t) * 3)
    Sparse matrix representing the collinearity constraint equations.

functions.name: cameramodels.calib.linear.coplanarity_w2c
documentation:
Construct a sparse matrix A

Construct a sparse matrix A that represents
coplanarity constraint equations.

Parameters
----------
Ra : array_like, shape (3, 3)
    Rotation matrix for the first set of triangles.
Rb : array_like, shape (3, 3)
    Rotation matrix for the second set of triangles.
na : array_like, shape (n, 3)
    Normal vectors of the first set of triangles.
nb : array_like, shape (n, 3)
    Normal vectors of the second set of triangles.
idx_t1 : array_like, shape (n_t1,)
    Indices of the first set of triangles.
idx_t2 : array_like, shape (n_t2,)
    Indices of the second set of triangles.
num_t : int
    Total number of triangles in the mesh.

Returns
-------
A : sparse matrix, shape (n, num_t * 3)
    Sparse matrix representing the coplanarity constraint equations.

functions.name: cameramodels.calib.linear.calib_linear
documentation:
Extrinsic camera calibration.

Performs linear camera calibration using the collinearity
and coplanarity constraints.
See Extrinsic Camera Calibration From a Moving Person
https://vision.ist.i.kyoto-u.ac.jp/research/calibperson/

Parameters:
-----------
v_CxNx3 : ndarray of shape (C, N, 3)
    Coordinates of 3D points in the world coordinate system.
n_CxMx3 : ndarray of shape (C, M, 3)
    Coordinates of corresponding 2D image points
    in the camera coordinate system.
compute_x : bool, optional
    Whether to compute the 3D coordinates of the points. Defaults to False.

Returns:
--------
R_w2c_list : ndarray of shape (C, 3, 3)
    Rotation matrices from world to camera coordinate system.
t_w2c_list : ndarray of shape (C, 3, 1)
    Translation vectors from world to camera coordinate system.
scale : float
    Global scale factor.
X2 : ndarray of shape (M, 3), optional
    If compute_x is True, returns the 3D coordinates of the points
    in the world coordinate system.

methods.name: cameramodels.stereo_camera.StereoCameraModel.Q
documentation:
Return Q matrix.

$$
Q = \left(     \begin{array}{cccc}       1 & 0 & 0 & -c_x \\       0 & 1 & 0 & -c_y \\       0 & 0 & 0 & f_x \\       0 & 0 & -1/T_x & (c_x - c'_{x})/T_x     \end{array} \right)
$$

# Returns

self.\_Q

: Q matrix.

methods.name: cameramodels.stereo_camera.StereoCameraModel.update_q
documentation:
Update variable fields of reprojection matrix

From Springer Handbook of Robotics, p. 524:

$$
P = \left(     \begin{array}{cccc}       f_x & 0 & c_x & 0 \\       0 & f_y & c_y & 0 \\       0 & 0 & 1 & 0     \end{array} \right)
$$

$$
P' = \left(     \begin{array}{cccc}       f_x & 0 & c'_x & f_x T_x \\       0 & f_y & c_y & 0 \\       0 & 0 & 1 & 0     \end{array} \right)
$$

where primed parameters are from the left projection matrix,
unprimed from the right.

```
[u   v 1]^T = P  * [x y z 1]^T
[(u-d) v 1]^T = P' * [x y z 1]^T
```

Combining the two equations above results in the following equation

```
[u v u-d 1]^T = [ Fx   0    Cx   0    ] * [ x y z 1]^T
                [ 0    Fy   Cy   0    ]
                [ Fx   0    Cx'  FxTx ]
                [ 0    0    1    0    ]
```

Subtracting the 3rd from from the first and inverting the expression
results in the following equation.

```
[x y z 1]^T = Q * [u v d 1]^T
```

Where Q is defined as

$$
Q = \left(     \begin{array}{cccc}       f_y T_x & 0 & 0 & -f_y c_x T_x \\       0 & f_x T_x & 0 & -f_x c_y T_x \\       0 & 0 & 0 & f_x f_y T_x \\       0 & 0 & - f_y & f_y (c_x - c'_x)     \end{array} \right)
$$

Using the assumption f_x = f_y Q can be simplified to the following.
But for compatibility with stereo cameras with different focal lengths
we will use the full Q matrix.

$$
Q = \left(     \begin{array}{cccc}       1 & 0 & 0 & -c_x \\       0 & 1 & 0 & -c_y \\       0 & 0 & 0 & f_x \\       0 & 0 & -1/T_x & (c_x - c'_{x})/T_x     \end{array} \right)
$$

$$
Disparity = x_{left} - x_{right}
$$

For compatibility with stereo cameras with different focal lengths
we will use the full Q matrix.

documentation: Checks that the translation vector is valid.

functions.name: cameramodels.math.wxyz2xyzw
documentation:
Convert quaternion [w, x, y, z] to [x, y, z, w] order.

# Parameters

quat

: quaternion [w, x, y, z]

# Returns

quaternion

: quaternion [x, y, z, w]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from skrobot.coordinates.math import wxyz2xyzw
>>> wxyz2xyzw([1, 2, 3, 4])
array([2, 3, 4, 1])
```

functions.name: cameramodels.math.xyzw2wxyz
documentation:
Convert quaternion [x, y, z, w] to [w, x, y, z] order.

# Parameters

quat

: quaternion [x, y, z, w]

# Returns

quaternion

: quaternion [w, x, y, z]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from skrobot.coordinates.math import xyzw2wxyz
>>> xyzw2wxyz([1, 2, 3, 4])
array([4, 1, 2, 3])
```

functions.name: cameramodels.math.matrix2quaternion
documentation:
Returns quaternion of given rotation matrix.

# Parameters

m

: 3x3 rotation matrix

# Returns

quaternion

: quaternion [w, x, y, z] order

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import numpy
>>> from skrobot.coordinates.math import matrix2quaternion
>>> matrix2quaternion(np.eye(3))
array([1., 0., 0., 0.])
```

functions.name: cameramodels.math.quaternion2matrix
documentation:
Returns matrix of given quaternion.

# Parameters

quaternion

: quaternion [w, x, y, z] order

normalize

: if normalize is True, input quaternion is normalized.

# Returns

rot

: 3x3 rotation matrix

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import numpy
>>> from skrobot.coordinates.math import quaternion2matrix
>>> quaternion2matrix([1, 0, 0, 0])
array([[1., 0., 0.],
       [0., 1., 0.],
       [0., 0., 1.]])
```

functions.name: cameramodels.math.rodrigues
documentation:
Rodrigues formula.

See: [Rodrigues' rotation formula - Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rodrigues%27_rotation_formula).

See: [Axis-angle representation - Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Axis%E2%80%93angle_representation).

# Parameters

axis

: [x, y, z] vector.
  You can give axis-angle representation to `axis` if `theta` is None.

theta: float or None (optional)

: radian. If None is given, calculate theta from axis.

# Returns

mat

: 3x3 rotation matrix

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import numpy
>>> from skrobot.coordinates.math import rodrigues
>>> rodrigues([1, 0, 0], 0)
array([[1., 0., 0.],
       [0., 1., 0.],
       [0., 0., 1.]])
>>> rodrigues([1, 1, 1], numpy.pi)
array([[-0.33333333,  0.66666667,  0.66666667],
       [ 0.66666667, -0.33333333,  0.66666667],
       [ 0.66666667,  0.66666667, -0.33333333]])
```

functions.name: cameramodels.math.rotation_angle
documentation:
Inverse Rodrigues formula Convert Rotation-Matirx to Axis-Angle.

Return theta and axis.
If given unit matrix, return None.

Parameters
----------
mat : numpy.ndarray
    rotation matrix, shape (3, 3)

Returns
-------
vec : numpy.ndarray
    The rotation vector of the given rotation matrix.

Notes
-----
This function uses the Rodrigues formula to calculate the rotation vector
from the given rotation matrix. The returned vector has the same direction
as the axis of rotation and its magnitude is equal to the rotation angle.

functions.name: cameramodels.math.calc_delta_rvec
documentation:
Calculate the rotation vector.

Calculate the rotation vector that represents the relative rotation
between two given rotation matrices.

Parameters
----------
gt_r : numpy.ndarray
    The ground truth rotation matrix.
estimate_r : numpy.ndarray
    The estimated rotation matrix.

Returns
-------
vec : numpy.ndarray
    The rotation vector that represents the relative rotation
    between the given rotation matrices.

functions.name: cameramodels.math.quaternion_norm
documentation:
Return the norm of quaternion.

# Parameters

q

: [w, x, y, z] order

# Returns

norm_q

: quaternion norm of q

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from skrobot.coordinates.math import quaternion_norm
>>> q = [1, 1, 1, 1]
>>> quaternion_norm(q)
2.0
>>> q = [0, 0.7071067811865476, 0, 0.7071067811865476]
>>> quaternion_norm(q)
1.0
```

functions.name: cameramodels.math.quaternion_normalize
documentation:
Return the normalized quaternion.

# Parameters

q

: [w, x, y, z] order

# Returns

normalized_q

: normalized quaternion

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from skrobot.coordinates.math import quaternion_normalize
>>> from skrobot.coordinates.math import quaternion_norm
>>> q = quaternion_normalize([1, 1, 1, 1])
>>> quaternion_norm(q)
1.0
```

Return Tx.

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel.K
documentation:
Intrinsic camera matrix for the raw (distorted) images.

$$
K = \left(     \begin{array}{ccc}       f_x & 0 & c_x \\       0 & f_y & c_y \\       0 & 0 & 1     \end{array} \right)
$$

Projects 3D points in the camera coordinate frame to 2D pixel
coordinates using the focal lengths (fx, fy) and principal point
(cx, cy).

# Returns

self.\_K

: 3x3 intrinsic matrix.

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel.K_inv
documentation:
Inverse of Intrinsic camera matrix for the raw (distorted) images.

$$
K^{-1} = \left(     \begin{array}{ccc}       1 / f_x & 0 & - c_x / f_x \\       0 & 1 / f_y & - c_y / f_y \\       0 & 0 & 1     \end{array} \right)
$$

Projects 3D points in the camera coordinate frame to 2D pixel
coordinates using the focal lengths (fx, fy) and principal point
(cx, cy).

# Returns

self.\_K

: 3x3 intrinsic matrix.

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel.P
documentation:
Projection camera_matrix

By convention, this matrix specifies the intrinsic
(camera) matrix of the processed (rectified) image.

$$
P = \left(     \begin{array}{cccc}       {f_x}' & 0 & {c_x}' & T_x \\       0 & {f_y}' & {c_y}' & T_y \\       0 & 0 & 1 & 0     \end{array} \right)
$$

# Returns

self.\_P

: 4x3 projection matrix.

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel.R
documentation:
Rectification matrix (stereo cameras only)

A rotation matrix aligning the camera coordinate system to the ideal
stereo image plane so that epipolar lines in both stereo images are
parallel.

Returns
-------
self._R : numpy.ndarray
    rectification matrix.

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel.D
documentation:
Property of distortion parameters

The distortion parameters, size depending on the distortion model.
For "plumb_bob", the 5 parameters are: (k1, k2, t1, t2, k3).

Returns
-------
self._D : numpy.ndarray
    distortion array.

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel.open3d_intrinsic
documentation:
Return open3d.camera.PinholeCameraIntrinsic instance.

Returns
-------
intrinsic : open3d.camera.PinholeCameraIntrinsic
    open3d PinholeCameraIntrinsic

Rectify input raw image.

-------
out_point : numpy.ndarray
    resized point.

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel.project_pixel_to_3d_ray
documentation:
Returns the ray vector

Returns the unit vector which passes from the camera center to
through rectified pixel (u, v),
using the camera $P$ matrix.
This is the inverse of {meth}`project3d_to_pixel`.

# Parameters

uv

: rectified pixel coordinates

normalize

: if True, return normalized ray vector (unit vector).

# Returns

ray_vector

: ray vector.

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel.batch_project_pixel_to_3d_ray
documentation:
Returns the ray vectors

This function is the batch version of
{meth}`project_pixel_to_3d_ray`.
Returns the unit vector which passes from the
camera center to through rectified pixel (u, v),
using the camera $P$ matrix.
This is the inverse of {meth}`batch_project3d_to_pixel`.
If depth is specified, return 3d points.

# Parameters

uv

: rectified pixel coordinates

depth

: depth value. If this value is specified,
  Return 3d points.

# Returns

ret

: calculated ray vectors or points(depth is given case).
  Shape of (batch_size, 3)

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel.project3d_to_pixel
documentation:
Returns the rectified pixel coordinates

Returns the rectified pixel coordinates (u, v) of the 3D point,
using the camera $P$ matrix.
This is the inverse of :meth `project_pixel_to_3d_ray`.

# Parameters

point

: 3D point (x, y, z)

# Returns

uv

: uv coordinates. If point is not in range of this camera model,
  return tuple(float('nan'), float('nan')).

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel.batch_project3d_to_pixel
documentation:
Return project uv coordinates points

Returns the rectified pixel coordinates (u, v) of the 3D points
using the camera $P$ matrix.
This is the inverse of {meth}`batch_project_pixel_to_3d_ray`.

# Parameters

points

: batch of xyz point (batch_size, 3)

project_valid_depth_only

: If True, return uvs which are in frame.

return_indices

: If this value and project_valid_depth_only are True,
  return valid indices.

# Returns

points

: shape of (batch_size, 2).

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel.points_to_depth
documentation:
Return depth image from 3D points.

Parameters
----------
points : numpy.ndarray
    batch of xyz point (batch_size, 3) or (height, width, 3).
depth_value : float
    default depth value.

Returns
-------
depth : numpy.ndarray
    projected depth image.

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel.depth_to_points
documentation:
Convert depth image to point clouds.

Parameters
----------
depth : numpy.ndarray
    depth image.

Returns
-------
points : numpy.ndarray
    return shape is (width, height, 3).

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel.get_view_frustum
documentation:
Return View Frustsum of this camera model.

# Parameters

max_depth

: max depth of frustsum.

translation

: translation vector

rotation

: rotation matrix

# Returns

view_frust_pts

: view frust points shape of (5, 3).

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from cameramodels import Xtion
>>> cameramodel = Xtion()
>>> cameramodel.get_view_frustum(max_depth=1.0)
array([[ 0.        ,  0.        ,  0.        ],
       [-0.41421356, -0.41421356,  1.        ],
       [-0.41421356,  0.41421356,  1.        ],
       [ 0.41421356,  0.41421356,  1.        ],
       [ 0.41421356, -0.41421356,  1.        ]])
```

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel.in_view_frustum
documentation:
Determine if points in the view frustum.

Parameters
----------
points : numpy.ndarray or list[tuple(float, float)]
    3D point (x, y, z).
max_depth : float
    max depth of frustsum.

Returns
-------
ret : numpy.ndarray or bool
    bool array. True indicates point in this view frustsum.

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel.flatten_uv
documentation:
Flattens uv coordinates to single dimensional tensor.

This is the inverse of {meth}`flattened_pixel_locations_to_uv`.

# Parameters

uv

: A pair of uv pixels. Shape of (batch_size, 2).
  [(u_1, v_1), (u_2, v_2) ..., (u_n, v_n)].

dtype

: data type. default is numpy.int64.

# Returns

ret

: Flattened uv tensor of shape (n, ).

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from cameramodels import PinholeCameraModel
>>> cm = PinholeCameraModel.from_fovy(45, 480, 640)
>>> cm.flatten_uv(np.array([(1, 0), (100, 1), (100, 2)]))
array([   1,  740, 1380])
```

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel.flattened_pixel_locations_to_uv
documentation:
Flattens pixel locations(single dimension tensor) to uv coordinates.

This is the inverse of {meth}`flatten_uv`.

# Parameters

flat_pixel_locations

: Flattened pixel locations.

# Returns

ret

: UV coordinates.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from cameramodels import PinholeCameraModel
>>> cm = PinholeCameraModel.from_fovy(45, 480, 640)
>>> flatten_uv = [1, 740, 1380]
>>> cm.flattened_pixel_locations_to_uv(flatten_uv)
array([[  1,   0],
       [100,   1],
       [100,   2]])
```

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel.dump
documentation:
Dump this camera's parameter to yaml file.

Parameters
----------
output_filepath : str or pathlib.Path
    output path
save_original : bool
    If `False`, save resized camera info.

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel.draw_roi
documentation:
Draw Region of Interest

Parameters
----------
bgr_img : numpy.ndarray
    input image.
color : tuple(int)
    RGB order color.
box_width : None or int
    box width. If `None`, automatically set from image size.
copy : bool
    If `True`, return copy image.
    If input image is gray image, this option will be ignored.

Returns
-------
img : numpy.ndarray
    ROI drawn image.

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel.points_in_roi
documentation:
Check if input points are in roi.

Parameters
----------
points : list[list[float, float]]
    [[x_1, y_1], [x_2, y_2] ..., [x_n, y_n]].

Returns
-------
result list[bool]
    True if the point is in roi, False otherwise.

methods.name: cameramodels.pinhole_camera.PinholeCameraModel._calc_resize_image_size_keeping_aspect_ratio
documentation:
Calculate resized image size keeping aspect ratio.

Parameters
----------
target_size : tuple(int or None)
    values of (width, height).
    If width or height is None,
    calculate it from aspect ratio.

Returns
-------
resized_size : tuple(int)
    resized target size.

A Pinhole Camera Model

more detail, see http://wiki.ros.org/image_pipeline/CameraInfo
http://docs.ros.org/api/sensor_msgs/html/msg/CameraInfo.html

Parameters
----------
image_height : int
    height of camera image.
image_width : int
    width of camera image.
K : numpy.ndarray
    3x3 intrinsic matrix.
P : numpy.ndarray
    3x4 projection matrix
R : numpy.ndarray
    3x3 rectification matrix.
D : numpy.ndarray
    distortion.
roi : None or list[float]
    [top, left, bottom, right] order.
tf_frame : None or str
    tf frame. This is for ROS compatibility.
stamp : None
    timestamp. This is for ROS compatibility.
distortion_model : str
    type of distortion model.
name : None or str
    name of this camera.
full_K : numpy.ndarray or None
    original intrinsic matrix of full resolution.
    If `None`, set copy of K.
full_P : numpy.ndarray or None
    original projection matrix of full resolution.
    If `None`, set copy of P.
full_height : int or None
    This value is indicating original image height.
full_width : int or None
    This value is indicating original image width.
